title: Preparing CCMB80 Chemically Competent Cells (TOP10/DHB10)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"80tvr","text":"This is the chemical transformation protocol used by Tom Knight and the Registry of Standard Biological Parts.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":110}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":53,"length":10},{"key":1,"offset":72,"length":37}],"data":{}},{"key":"2mfdq","text":"It was adapted from the openwetware version.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":44}],"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"offset":24,"length":19}],"data":{}},{"key":"6l4a5","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fj1i","text":"This protocol is a variant of the Hanahan protocol [1] using CCMB80 buffer for DH10B, TOP10 and MachI strains. It builds on Example 2 of the  Bloom05 patent as well. This protocol has been tested on TOP10, MachI and BL21(DE3) cells. See Bacterial Transformation for a more general discussion of other techniques. The  Jesse '464 patent describes using this buffer for DH5α cells. The Bloom04 patent describes the use of essentially the same protocol for the Invitrogen Mach 1 cells.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":3,"offset":52,"length":1},{"key":4,"offset":142,"length":14},{"key":5,"offset":216,"length":9},{"key":6,"offset":237,"length":24},{"key":7,"offset":318,"length":17},{"key":8,"offset":384,"length":7}],"data":{}},{"key":"5p5h2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bd4qs","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"7194892E58D211F0B1B50A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://web.archive.org/web/20250118205335/https://openwetware.org/wiki/User:Tk"}},"1":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"7194896658D211F0B1B50A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://web.archive.org/web/20250118205335/http://partsregistry.org"}},"2":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"99411E52EECA4232952716885EA69782","url":"https://openwetware.org/wiki/TOP10_chemically_competent_cells"}},"3":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"719489EB58D211F0B1B50A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://web.archive.org/web/20250118205335/https://openwetware.org/wiki/TOP10_chemically_competent_cells#bibkey_Hanahan91"}},"4":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"71948A2A58D211F0B1B50A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://web.archive.org/web/20250118205335/https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/oww-files-public/b/bd/Pat6855494.pdf"}},"5":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"71948A6458D211F0B1B50A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://web.archive.org/web/20250118205335/https://openwetware.org/wiki/Talk:TOP10_chemically_competent_cells"}},"6":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"71948A9C58D211F0B1B50A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://web.archive.org/web/20250118205335/https://openwetware.org/wiki/Bacterial_Transformation"}},"7":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"7194889558D211F0B1B50A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://web.archive.org/web/20250118205335/https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/oww-files-public/0/0c/Pat6960464.pdf"}},"8":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"719488EA58D211F0B1B50A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://web.archive.org/web/20250118205335/https://s3-us-west-2.amazonaws.com/oww-files-public/c/c2/Pat6709852.pdf"}}}}

Teknova","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"link","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"A261822C58D211F0B1B50A58A9FEAC02","url":"https://web.archive.org/web/20250118205335/https://openwetware.org/wiki/SOB"}}}}
steps: Plate 80 µL on agar plates with the appropriate antibiotic. You can use spreaders or sterile 3.5 mm glass beads to evenly spread out the cells. Good cells should yield around 100 - 400 colonies Transformation efficiency is (dilution factor=15) x colony count x 10 5 /µgDNA We expect that the transformation efficiency should be between 5x10 8 and 5x10 9 cfu/µgDNA

Incubate at 37 °C for 01:00:00 in 2 mL centrifuge tubes, ideally on a lab rotator. Using 2 mL centrifuge tubes for transformation and regrowth works well because the small volumes flow well when rotated, increasing aeration. Ampicillin and kanamycin resistant plasmids require 01:00:00 growth whereas chloramphenicol and tetracycline resistant plasmids may benefit from 02:00:00 to increase transformation efficiency and colony yield.
Add 250 µL SOC media to each tube.
Heat shock tubes in a water bath at 42 °C for 00:01:00
Incubate tubes On ice 00:30:00

Transform 50 µL of cells with 1 µL of 10 pg/μl pUC19 plasmid in a 1.5 ml or 2 ml microcentrifuge tube. 10 pg/μl or 10 -5 μg/μl pUC19 can be made by diluting 1 µL of NEB pUC19 plasmid (1 μg/μl, NEB part number N3401S) into 100 mL of TE buffer.

Store at -80 °C indefinitely. Flash freezing does not appear to be necessary. Thawing and refreezing partially used cell aliquots dramatically reduces transformation efficiency by about 3x the first time, and about 6x total after several freeze/thaw cycles.
Aliquot to chilled screw top 2 mL vials or 50 µL into chilled microtiter plates
Add chilled CCMB80 to yield a final OD of 1.0-1.5 in this test.
Test OD of a mixture of 200 µL SOC media and 50 µL of the resuspended cells.
Resuspend in 10 mL of ice cold CCMB80 buffer.
Centrifuge again 3000 rpm, 4 °C, 20 minutes and discard supernatant as described above in go to step #1 (then go to step #1 , do not resuspend as per go to step #1 )
Incubate On ice for 00:20:00
Gently resuspend in 80 mL of ice cold CCMB80 buffer. Sometimes this is necessarily less than completely gentle. It still works.
Discard supernatant by pouring out slowly and then gently using a seropipette to remove the remaining supernatant.

Centrifuge the culture at 3000 rpm, 4 °C, 20 minutes in a flat bottom centrifuge bottle. Flat bottom centrifuge tubes make the fragile cells much easier to resuspend. It is often easier to resuspend pellets by mixing before adding large amounts of buffer

Inoculate 250 mL of SOB medium with 1 mL vial of seed stock and grow at 20 °C or Room temperature to an OD 600nm of 0.3 for approximately 16:00:00 , measuring OD with a spectrophotometer or plate reader every 30-60 min from around 12 hours until you have a good sense of how long to leave the culture. You can adjust the incubation temperature somewhat to fit your schedule. Room temperature will work; controlling the temperature makes this a more reproducible process, but is not essential. Aim for lower, not higher OD if you can't hit the OD600nm of 0.3 mark.
Place in -80 °C freezer indefinitely.
[Optional] Place tubes into a zip lock bag, immerse bag into a dry ice/ethanol bath for 00:05:00
Aliquot 1 mL samples to Nunc cryotubes
Add glycerol to 15 % (v/v)
Pick single colonies into 2 mL of SOB medium and shake overnight at 23 °C or Room temperature
Streak TOP10/DH10B cells on an SOB plate and grow for single colonies at 23 °C or Room temperature
Prechill plasticware and glassware Prechill 250 mL L 250 mL centrifuge tubes and screw cap tubes before use by placing them in a -20 °C freezer or On ice .

Prepare detergent-free glassware Autoclave necessary glassware filled 75% with deionised water. Detergent is a major inhibitor of competent cell growth and transformation. Media and buffers should be prepared in detergent free glassware and cultures grown up in detergent free glassware. The easiest way to do this is to avoid washing glassware, and simply rinse it out. Autoclaving glassware filled 75% with DI water is an effective way to remove most detergent residue.

This protocol outlines the preparation of a CCMB80 buffer with specific concentrations of potassium acetate, calcium chloride, manganese chloride, magnesium chloride, and glycerol. The buffer is adjusted to a pH of pH 6.4 to pH 7.0 and is sterile filtered for storage at 4 °C . Total duration for the entire protocol is approximately 00:50:00 . The presence of slight dark precipitate does not affect the buffer's functionality. Wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) including gloves, goggles, and lab coat. Handle all reagents with care, particularly manganese chloride and hydrochloric acid, as they are toxic and corrosive. The buffer should be a clear solution with no precipitate. If precipitation occurs during pH adjustment, ensure pH is adjusted downwards only; avoiding raising pH should prevent manganese dioxide precipitation. If the buffer does not dissolve completely, ensure adequate stirring and check the purity of reagents.
Buffer Preparation Summary %s %s
Quality Control %s %s
Summary of Stock Reagents %s %s
Equipment pH meter Magnetic stirrer Sterile filtration unit Graduated cylinder Weighing balance Beakers (200 ml)

Buffer Preparation Protocol In a 200 mL beaker, add 50 mL of distilled water, then 1 mL of 1 Molarity (M) potassium acetate solution. Add 1.18 g of calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl 2 ·2H 2 O), 0.4 g of manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl 2 ·4H 2 O), and 0.2 g of magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl 2 ·6H 2 O) to the beaker. Add 10 mL of glycerol. Add distilled water to bring the total volume to 100 mL Stir the solution using a magnetic stirrer until all solids are completely dissolved.

pH Adjustment Measure the pH of the solution using a pH meter. If the pH is above pH 7.0 , slowly add 0.1 Molarity (M) HCl dropwise while stirring until the pH reaches 6.4. If the pH is below 7.0, no adjustment is necessary. Adjusting the pH upwards will precipitate manganese dioxide; ensure to only adjust downwards if necessary.

Sterile Filtration Filter the prepared buffer solution through a sterile filtration unit to ensure sterility. Collect the filtered buffer in a sterile container. Ensure all equipment used for filtration is sterile to prevent contamination.
Place 100 mL CCMB80 buffer On ice or in the -20 °C freezer for a short time, then on ice.
Incubate On ice for 00:03:00
creator: Jiayi Hou
